content.text: Metrics & Monitoring for Python Data Pipelines

Observability is the practice of understanding what is happening inside a system by examining the signals it produces from the outside. In data pipelines, observability is not optional — it is the difference between discovering a failure from an angry stakeholder at 9am versus catching a silent data quality drift at 3am before it ever reaches production.

are numeric measurements sampled or aggregated over time that describe the health and behavior of a pipeline. Examples: records processed per second, error rate, lag behind a source system, memory usage, job duration.
is the continuous collection, storage, and alerting on those metrics so that human operators (or automated systems) can detect and respond to anomalies.
is the broader discipline that encompasses metrics, logs, and traces together. For data pipelines, it answers three questions:
A pipeline that silently drops 5% of records while appearing to run normally is a classic observability failure. Good metrics catch this.
Data pipelines have unique failure modes that infrastructure metrics alone cannot detect:
A pipeline runs to completion but writes zero rows (empty source file, silent filter bug).
A pipeline runs to completion but writes wrong data (upstream schema change, encoding error).
A pipeline runs but is 4 hours late because a single slow partition blocked the entire job.
Row counts look correct but a join produced duplicate keys.
None of these appear in CPU or memory graphs. You need
layered on top of infrastructure metrics.
The business impact is severe: stale dashboards, wrong KPIs, incorrect ML training data, failed compliance audits, and lost revenue — all from pipelines that appeared "green."
Core Metric Categories for Data Pipelines
How much data is the pipeline moving?
For batch pipelines: number of batches
Records that failed schema or business rule checks
Fraction of nulls in key fields
Wall-clock time for a full run
How far behind the pipeline is from the source
Records that could not be processed
Usually collected by infrastructure agents, but can be emitted from within Python:
endpoint; the Prometheus server scrapes it on a schedule. Works well for long-running services.
The pipeline pushes metric values to a collector as they occur. Works well for short-lived batch jobs that do not run long enough to be scraped.
How to Implement — Step by Step

pip install prometheus_client   # Prometheus (pull model)
pip install statsd              # StatsD (push model)
pip install datadog             # Datadog agent
pip install psutil              # System/process metrics
Create a single module that owns all metric objects. This prevents duplicate registration errors and centralizes naming.

# metrics.py
from prometheus_client import (
    Counter, Gauge, Histogram, Summary, CollectorRegistry, push_to_gateway
)

# Use a custom registry for testability (avoids polluting the default registry)
REGISTRY = CollectorRegistry()

# --- Throughput ---
records_read = Counter(
    "pipeline_records_read_total",
    "Total records read from source",
    ["pipeline_name", "source"],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

records_written = Counter(
    "pipeline_records_written_total",
    "Total records successfully written to sink",
    ["pipeline_name", "destination"],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

records_failed = Counter(
    "pipeline_records_failed_total",
    "Total records that failed processing",
    ["pipeline_name", "reason"],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

# --- Latency ---
pipeline_duration = Histogram(
    "pipeline_duration_seconds",
    "End-to-end pipeline duration in seconds",
    ["pipeline_name"],
    buckets=[30, 60, 120, 300, 600, 1800, 3600],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

stage_duration = Histogram(
    "pipeline_stage_duration_seconds",
    "Duration of a single pipeline stage",
    ["pipeline_name", "stage"],
    buckets=[1, 5, 10, 30, 60, 120, 300],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

# --- Data Quality ---
validation_failures = Counter(
    "pipeline_validation_failures_total",
    "Records failing data quality checks",
    ["pipeline_name", "check_name"],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

# --- State ---
pipeline_last_success_timestamp = Gauge(
    "pipeline_last_success_timestamp_seconds",
    "Unix timestamp of the last successful pipeline run",
    ["pipeline_name"],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

records_in_dead_letter = Gauge(
    "pipeline_dead_letter_queue_size",
    "Number of records in the dead-letter queue",
    ["pipeline_name"],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)

# --- Resource ---
memory_rss = Gauge(
    "pipeline_memory_rss_bytes",
    "Resident set size in bytes",
    ["pipeline_name"],
    registry=REGISTRY,
)
Wrap pipeline logic with metric collection. The cleanest pattern is a
For batch jobs (push to Prometheus Pushgateway):

from prometheus_client import push_to_gateway
from metrics import REGISTRY

def push_metrics():
    push_to_gateway(
        "localhost:9091",           # Pushgateway address
        job="user_events_pipeline",
        registry=REGISTRY,
    )

# Call at end of run_pipeline()
push_metrics()

from prometheus_client import start_http_server
from metrics import REGISTRY

# In your service entry point:
start_http_server(port=8000, registry=REGISTRY)
# Prometheus will scrape http://localhost:8000/metrics

import statsd

client = statsd.StatsClient(host="localhost", port=8125, prefix="pipeline.user_events")

# In your pipeline:
client.incr("records_read")
client.incr("records_failed")
client.timing("stage.extract_ms", elapsed_ms)
client.gauge("memory_rss_bytes", rss)
. Use structured logging so logs are machine-parseable.
JSON logs can be shipped to Elasticsearch, Loki, or CloudWatch Logs and queried just like a database.
A nightly pipeline processes 20 million e-commerce order events from S3, validates them, enriches with product catalog data from PostgreSQL, and writes to a Redshift data warehouse.
Runs as a Kubernetes Job triggered by Apache Airflow at 02:00 UTC
Prometheus Pushgateway receives metrics on job completion
Grafana dashboard shows: records read/written, failure rate, stage durations, memory usage
AlertManager fires a PagerDuty alert if:
is older than 6 hours (pipeline stopped running) -
(job running more than 1 hour — SLA breach)
What the metrics revealed in practice:
A new upstream team changed the
field from integer to string. The pipeline's strict type validation silently rejected 8% of records. The
counter spiked. Alerted in 12 minutes instead of being found the next morning.

jumped from 120s to 900s. A Histogram percentile query (p99 duration) in Grafana showed the change precisely correlated with the deployment. The new library version had disabled connection pooling by default.

climbed monotonically across nightly runs. Each run leaked ~200MB. The gauge trend in Grafana made this obvious after 3 runs. The cause was a cached reference to a large in-memory lookup table that was never released.
Prometheus Metric Types — Choosing the Right One
Total records, total errors, total retries
A value that can go up or down
Current queue size, memory usage, lag
Distribution of observed values with configurable buckets
Similar to Histogram but computed client-side
for "how many times did X happen." Use
for "what is the current state of X." Use
for "how long did X take" or "how big is X."
for a value that should only ever increase
(like total records processed), because a gauge can reset to zero, and that reset looks identical to a legitimate zero. Use
Not every metric should produce an alert. Alert fatigue is real and dangerous — if alerts fire too often for non-urgent issues, operators start ignoring them.
The Four Golden Signals (from Google SRE)
Originally for services but fully applicable to data pipelines:
— How long is the pipeline taking? Alert if
— Is the pipeline processing the expected volume? Alert if
— Is the error rate above threshold? Alert if
— Is the pipeline nearing capacity? Alert if

# alerts.yml
groups:
  - name: data_pipeline_alerts
    rules:

      - alert: PipelineNotRunning
        # Fires if the pipeline hasn't succeeded in 6 hours
        expr: |
          (time() - pipeline_last_success_timestamp_seconds{pipeline_name="user_events"}) > 21600
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Pipeline {{ $labels.pipeline_name }} has not run successfully in 6 hours"

      - alert: HighRecordFailureRate
        # Fires if more than 2% of records are failing
        expr: |
          rate(pipeline_records_failed_total[5m])
          /
          rate(pipeline_records_read_total[5m]) > 0.02
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.pipeline_name }} record failure rate above 2%"
          description: "Current rate: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"

      - alert: PipelineDurationSLABreach
        # Fires if a pipeline run exceeds 60 minutes (p95)
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            rate(pipeline_duration_seconds_bucket{pipeline_name="user_events"}[1h])
          ) > 3600
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Pipeline {{ $labels.pipeline_name }} p95 duration exceeded 1 hour"

      - alert: PipelineMemoryHigh
        expr: |
          pipeline_memory_rss_bytes > 3e9   # 3 GB
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Pipeline memory usage above 3GB"
Infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, duration) tell you whether the pipeline
. Data quality metrics tell you whether it
. This is the layer most teams under-invest in.
Records that fail processing should never be silently dropped. They should be written to a dead-letter destination for later inspection, reprocessing, or alerting.
CPU and memory being normal does not mean the pipeline is correct. Always add domain-level metrics: records read, records written, validation failures.
Gauges can be set to any value including zero, which creates false alarms or hides real resets. Use
for anything that should only increase.
3. Swallowing exceptions without incrementing error metrics.
means failures are invisible. Always pair exception handling with a metric increment.
The most important single metric for a batch pipeline is
. Without it, you cannot alert on "pipeline stopped running." This is the pipeline equivalent of a heartbeat.
Too many alerts create alert fatigue. Alert only on customer-impacting conditions: pipeline not running, data loss above threshold, SLA duration breach. Use dashboards for everything else.

In Prometheus, never use high-cardinality values (user IDs, file paths, timestamps) as label values. Each unique label combination is a separate time series, and millions of time series will crash Prometheus. Use
Metric code is real code. Test it. Use a custom
in tests so you can assert on counter values without polluting the global default registry.

# test_metrics.py
from prometheus_client import CollectorRegistry, Counter

def test_records_failed_increments():
    test_registry = CollectorRegistry()
    counter = Counter(
        "test_records_failed_total", "test", ["reason"], registry=test_registry
    )
    counter.labels(reason="null_user_id").inc()
    counter.labels(reason="null_user_id").inc()

    # Get the current value
    value = test_registry.get_sample_value(
        "test_records_failed_total", {"reason": "null_user_id"}
    )
    assert value == 2.0
Every metric should carry at least
as a label so Grafana dashboards can filter and aggregate across multiple pipelines.
can jump backward due to NTP corrections.
A pipeline that fails should still push its metrics (records read so far, records failed, failure reason). Wrap the metric push in a

def run_pipeline():
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        do_work()
    except Exception:
        records_failed.labels(pipeline_name=PIPELINE_NAME, reason="fatal").inc()
        raise
    finally:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        pipeline_duration.labels(pipeline_name=PIPELINE_NAME).observe(elapsed)
        push_metrics()   # Always push, even on failure

The default Prometheus histogram buckets (0.005, 0.01, 0.025,..., 10 seconds) are designed for HTTP request latency. For pipelines that run for minutes or hours, define custom buckets that match your actual SLAs.
metric hides where time is spent. Instrument each stage (extract, validate, enrich, load) separately so you can pinpoint bottlenecks.
Correlate metrics with logs via trace IDs.
(UUID) to every log line and include it in metric labels where cardinality allows (or just log it and use it to find logs from a given run).

import uuid
RUN_ID = str(uuid.uuid4())

logger.info("Pipeline started", extra={"run_id": RUN_ID, "pipeline": PIPELINE_NAME})
Industry standard; supports pull and push
Simple UDP push; works with Datadog, Graphite
Emerging standard; exports to any backend
Works with Prometheus; routes to PagerDuty/Slack
Pairs well with Prometheus; label-based querying
Heavier; better for unstructured log search
No infrastructure to manage; push model
Tight integration with GCP data services
Rich DQ validation framework; integrates with pipelines
SQL-level data quality for warehouse pipelines
Column-level tests: not-null, unique, accepted values
Open source, powerful query language (PromQL), great for Kubernetes, large ecosystem
Requires running Prometheus server; pull model needs Pushgateway for batch jobs
Fully managed, unified metrics + logs + traces + APM, excellent UI
Zero infrastructure; tight AWS integration; good for Lambda/Glue/EMR pipelines
Costly at high metric volume; weaker query language
Graphite is aging; limited label/tag support
Vendor-neutral; one SDK for metrics + traces + logs
Ecosystem still maturing; more complex setup
Not queryable, not aggregatable, no alerting, not production-grade
For new Python pipeline projects, the recommended baseline is
OpenTelemetry SDK with a managed backend
(Datadog, Honeycomb, New Relic) for cloud-native deployments.
Metrics and monitoring for data pipelines require two layers:
The single most important metric for any batch pipeline is
— it enables alerting on pipelines that have silently stopped running.
for distributions and durations. Never use a
Pair metrics with structured JSON logging so that when an alert fires, the logs provide the detailed context needed to diagnose and fix the problem quickly.
Dead-letter queues ensure that failed records are never silently dropped — they are captured, counted, and recoverable.
Alert only on customer-impacting conditions (pipeline down, data loss, SLA breach); use dashboards for everything else to avoid alert fatigue.
Histogram buckets must be tuned to your actual pipeline durations — default Prometheus buckets are designed for millisecond HTTP latency, not minute-scale batch jobs.
